questions.question: Define a tree. Explain key terminology: root, leaf, internal node, height, depth, and degree. (5 marks)
keyPoints: Tree: Non-linear hierarchical data structure with nodes connected by edges.
Root: Topmost node with no parent. Leaf: Node with no children (degree 0).
Internal node: Node with at least one child. Degree: Number of children of a node.
Height: Edges on longest path from root to leaf. Depth: Edges from root to that node.

questions.question: Explain the three tree traversal methods with an example binary tree. (5 marks)
keyPoints: Inorder (Left-Root-Right): Visits left subtree, root, then right subtree.
Preorder (Root-Left-Right): Visits root, left subtree, then right subtree.
Postorder (Left-Right-Root): Visits left subtree, right subtree, then root.
Example with a small tree demonstrating each traversal order.

questions.question: What is a binary tree? Explain different types of binary trees with examples and properties. (10 marks)
keyPoints: Binary tree: Each node has at most two children (left and right).
Full binary tree: Every node has 0 or 2 children.
Complete binary tree: All levels fully filled except possibly the last, filled left to right.
Perfect binary tree: All internal nodes have two children, all leaves at same level.
Skewed binary tree: All nodes have only left (or only right) children.

questions.question: Compare trees with linear data structures. Why are trees needed? (5 marks)
keyPoints: Linear structures: Sequential arrangement, O(n) search. Trees: Hierarchical arrangement, O(log n) search in balanced trees.
Trees can represent hierarchical relationships (file systems, org charts).
BST enables efficient O(log n) search, insert, and delete.
Applications: Database indexing, expression evaluation, file systems.
